seo.title: Government Content Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for content generation AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for government content generation carries extraordinary stakes: misinformation, classified data exposure, or non-compliant outputs can trigger congressional investigations, contract debarment, and irreversible damage to public trust. Federal agencies must satisfy overlapping mandates from FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI before any LLM-powered content tool touches agency workflows. This checklist provides concrete, regulation-aligned controls to ensure your AI content system is secure, explainable, and audit-ready from day one.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Security Controls
sections.description: Ensure the AI content pipeline never ingests, processes, or outputs classified or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) through external LLM endpoints, and that all data flows comply with FedRAMP and FISMA requirements.

items.label: Classify all input data before LLM submission
items.description: Implement an automated pre-processing step that scans every content brief, prompt, and source document against your agency's data classification taxonomy (Unclassified, CUI, Confidential, Secret) and blocks any non-Unclassified content from reaching external LLM APIs.

items.label: Enforce FedRAMP-authorized LLM endpoints only
items.description: Maintain an approved vendor list of FedRAMP High or Moderate authorized AI providers and configure network egress rules to block API calls to any non-authorized endpoint. Review and update this list quarterly.

items.label: Implement data-at-rest and in-transit encryption per NIST 800-53 SC-28
items.description: Ensure all content drafts, prompt logs, and model outputs are encrypted using FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules both in storage and during transmission between agency systems and LLM endpoints.

items.label: Configure prompt injection and data exfiltration guardrails
items.description: Deploy input/output filtering layers that detect and block prompt injection attempts, prevent the model from echoing back sensitive context, and strip any PII or agency-internal metadata from generated content before it reaches end users.

items.label: Establish a CUI handling procedure for AI-assisted drafts
items.description: Create a written Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) that defines how human editors must handle AI-generated drafts that may reference CUI sources, including mandatory review checkpoints before publication.

items.label: Log all LLM API calls with immutable audit trails
items.description: Capture and store every prompt, model response, user ID, timestamp, and classification label in an immutable log system compliant with NIST 800-53 AU-9. Retain logs for a minimum of three years per FISMA requirements.

items.label: Conduct quarterly penetration testing on the content generation pipeline
items.description: Schedule red-team exercises specifically targeting the AI content workflow, including attempts to extract training data, bypass classification filters, and inject malicious instructions through editorial inputs.

sections.heading: Algorithmic Impact Assessment & Explainability
sections.description: Federal regulations require that AI systems affecting public-facing content undergo documented impact assessments and that any AI-assisted decision influencing citizens remains explainable and appealable.

items.label: Complete a formal Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before deployment
items.description: Document the AI system's purpose, training data sources, known biases, failure modes, and potential civil liberties impacts using the agency's AIA template. Obtain sign-off from the Chief AI Officer and General Counsel before go-live.

items.label: Implement content provenance tagging on all AI-generated outputs
items.description: Automatically append a machine-readable metadata tag to every AI-generated content piece indicating the model version, generation timestamp, human reviewer ID, and confidence score so downstream systems and auditors can trace content origin.

items.label: Build a citizen-facing disclosure statement for AI-assisted content
items.description: Draft and publish a plain-language disclosure on all agency web properties that use AI-generated content, explaining what the AI does, what data it uses, and how citizens can request human review of any AI-produced communication that affects them.

items.label: Create an appeals workflow for AI-influenced public communications
items.description: Establish a documented process by which citizens or stakeholders can formally challenge AI-generated content that affects their rights or benefits, including a designated human reviewer and a 30-day response SLA.

items.label: Document model decision logic for editorial guardrails
items.description: Maintain a human-readable specification of all brand voice rules, content filters, and editorial guidelines encoded into the AI system so that any output can be traced back to a specific rule or policy without requiring ML expertise.

items.label: Conduct bias audits on generated content across demographic groups
items.description: Run quarterly evaluations using representative demographic test sets to detect disparate language, framing, or omission patterns in AI-generated content that could constitute discriminatory communication toward protected groups.

items.label: Maintain a model card and system card updated with each model version change
items.description: Keep a current model card documenting intended use, out-of-scope uses, performance benchmarks, and known limitations. Update this document within 5 business days of any model version upgrade or fine-tuning event.

sections.heading: Content Verification & Editorial Quality Controls
sections.description: Government content must meet rigorous standards for factual accuracy, originality, brand consistency, and accessibility. Automated verification pipelines must be validated and human oversight must be mandatory before publication.

items.label: Integrate automated fact-checking against authoritative government data sources
items.description: Connect the content generation pipeline to verified data feeds (e.g., Data.gov, agency databases, Federal Register) and flag any AI-generated claim that cannot be corroborated by an authoritative source before it reaches a human editor.

items.label: Enforce mandatory human editorial review before any public-facing publication
items.description: Implement a workflow gate that requires a credentialed human editor to approve every AI-generated piece before it is published to any government website, press release system, or social media channel. No fully autonomous publication is permitted.

items.label: Run originality checks using plagiarism detection integrated with federal document repositories
items.description: Submit all AI-generated drafts to a plagiarism detection tool configured with access to federal document repositories, prior agency publications, and public web content to ensure outputs do not reproduce copyrighted or previously published material without attribution.

items.label: Validate Section 508 accessibility compliance on all generated web content
items.description: Run every AI-generated HTML or document output through an automated Section 508 / WCAG 2.1 AA compliance checker (e.g., axe, WAVE) and resolve all critical and serious violations before editorial review.

items.label: Enforce agency brand voice and terminology standards via a controlled vocabulary layer
items.description: Maintain a machine-readable agency style guide and controlled vocabulary list that is applied as a post-processing filter to all AI outputs, automatically flagging or replacing non-compliant terminology, tone deviations, and unauthorized acronyms.

items.label: Implement reading level and plain language scoring per Plain Writing Act requirements
items.description: Automatically score all AI-generated public communications using a Flesch-Kincaid or equivalent readability metric and flag content that exceeds an 8th-grade reading level for revision before publication.

items.label: Validate SEO metadata accuracy and policy compliance before publishing
items.description: Check that AI-generated meta titles, descriptions, and structured data accurately reflect content, do not contain misleading claims, and comply with agency digital strategy guidelines before content is indexed.

items.label: Maintain a content version history with diff tracking for all AI-assisted documents
items.description: Store every draft iteration of AI-generated content with a full diff log showing what the AI produced versus what human editors changed, enabling post-publication audits and FOIA-responsive records management.

sections.heading: Governance, Training & Incident Response
sections.description: Sustainable AI content operations in government require clear ownership, trained personnel, and a tested incident response plan that addresses AI-specific failure modes including hallucination, data leakage, and adversarial manipulation.

items.label: Designate an AI Content Governance Officer with documented authority
items.description: Formally appoint a named individual responsible for AI content policy compliance, vendor oversight, and incident escalation. Document this role in the agency's AI governance charter and ensure the officer has authority to halt AI content operations if a critical risk is identified.

items.label: Develop and test an AI-specific incident response playbook
items.description: Create a written incident response plan covering AI hallucination events, data classification violations, adversarial prompt injection, and unauthorized content publication. Conduct a tabletop exercise with all stakeholders at least once before deployment and annually thereafter.

items.label: Complete role-based AI literacy training for all content staff before system access
items.description: Require all editors, communications officers, and IT staff who interact with the AI content system to complete a role-specific training module covering responsible use, classification rules, hallucination risks, and escalation procedures. Track completion in the agency LMS.

items.label: Establish a model change management process with security review gates
items.description: Define a formal change management procedure requiring a security impact analysis, updated AIA review, and re-authorization approval from the Authorizing Official before any LLM model version upgrade, fine-tuning event, or new capability is enabled in production.

items.label: Implement continuous monitoring dashboards for content quality and security metrics
items.description: Deploy real-time dashboards tracking key metrics including hallucination rate, fact-check failure rate, classification filter trigger rate, Section 508 violation rate, and human override frequency. Review weekly and report monthly to agency leadership.

items.label: Conduct annual third-party audits of the AI content system against NIST AI RMF
items.description: Engage an independent assessor to evaluate the AI content system against the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions annually, and publish a summary of findings in the agency's AI transparency report.

items.label: Establish a public feedback mechanism for AI-generated content quality
items.description: Provide a clearly labeled feedback button on all AI-assisted public content pages allowing citizens to report inaccuracies, accessibility issues, or inappropriate content. Route submissions to the AI Content Governance Officer with a 5-business-day acknowledgment SLA.

notes.note: Any cloud-based LLM service used for government content generation must hold a current FedRAMP Authorization to Operate (ATO) at the impact level matching your agency's data sensitivity (Moderate or High). FISMA requires the AI system itself to be included in your agency's system inventory and covered by a continuous monitoring program with annual security assessments.

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls SA-11, SI-10, and AU-12 directly apply to AI content systems, requiring developer security testing, input validation, and comprehensive audit logging. Agencies should additionally align with the NIST AI RMF to document risk categorization, impact assessments, and ongoing measurement of AI content system performance and trustworthiness.

notes.regulation: Executive Order on AI (EO 14110) & OMB M-24-10
notes.note: EO 14110 and OMB Memorandum M-24-10 require federal agencies to designate a Chief AI Officer, inventory all AI use cases, complete rights-impacting and safety-impacting AI assessments, and report high-impact AI deployments to OMB. A public-facing AI content generation system likely qualifies as rights-impacting and must meet minimum practice requirements including human oversight, ongoing monitoring, and fallback procedures.

All content published on federal websites or distributed electronically must conform to Section 508 standards, which incorporate WCAG 2.1 Level AA. AI-generated content is not exempt: agencies remain legally responsible for accessibility compliance regardless of how content was produced. Automated accessibility validation must be embedded in the pre-publication workflow, and human reviewers must be trained to identify and remediate AI-generated accessibility failures.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI content pipeline against FedRAMP, FISMA, and Executive Order requirements—so your agency stays audit-ready and mission-focused without manual compliance overhead.
